elements.sent-2411-001: The search for heavy $W'$ bosons in their decay modes to a lepton and a heavy neutrino offers a promising avenue for probing new physics beyond the Standard Model.
elements.sent-2411-002: This work focuses on such a signature with an energetic lepton plus a fat jet, originating from the heavy neutrino and containing a lepton.
elements.sent-2411-003: We have employed the jet substructure techniques to isolate the embedded lepton as a subjet of the fat jet.
elements.sent-2411-004: The Lepton Subjet Fraction ($LSF$) and Lepton Mass Drop ($LMD$) variables constructed from the lepton subjet help in separating the signal region from the background.
elements.sent-2411-005: We further study the polarization properties of the $W'$ coupling to the lepton and heavy neutrino through the decay products of the neutrino.
elements.sent-2411-006: Instead of relying on a specific model, we employ generic couplings and explore the discrimination power.
elements.sent-2411-007: Jet substructure-based angular variables $z_\ell$, $z_\theta$, and $z_k$ are combined to form BDT scores to obtain better separation power between left-chiral ($V-A$) and right-chiral ($V+A$) coupling configurations.
elements.sent-2411-008: By using $CL_s$ type profile likelihood estimator, we could achieve $\sim$\;2$\sigma$ -- 3$\sigma$ significance of excluding one coupling configuration in favour of the other.
elements.sent-2411-009: The triumph of the Standard Model (SM) of particle physics was marked by the observation of a 125-GeV scalar particle, closely resembling the SM Higgs boson, by CMS and ATLAS collaborations at the LHC~\cite{CMS:2012qbp,ATLAS:2012yve .}
elements.sent-2411-010: Therefore, there is no denying the success of the SM, which has become the cornerstone of our understanding of fundamental interactions between the elementary particles seen in nature.
elements.sent-2411-011: Despite its success, several theoretical and experimental indications suggest the need for new physics beyond the Standard Model (BSM).
elements.sent-2411-012: From the theoretical perspective, we do not have a full understanding of the hierarchy of the masses of various flavours of the SM fermions, nor do we understand the lightness of the Higgs boson.
elements.sent-2411-013: These are referred to as the flavour hierarchy~\cite{Froggatt:1978nt and naturalness~\cite{MORRISSEY20121,Farina:2013mla,deGouvea:2014xba problems of the SM, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-014: More pressing, from the experimental side, the SM does not have any explanation for the masses and mixing of the three neutrino flavours~\cite{K2K:2002icj,PhysRevLett.90.021802,RevModPhys.88.030501, RevModPhys.88.030502,Esteban:2020cvm,deSalas:2020pgw .}
elements.sent-2411-015: At the same time, on the cosmological and astrophysical side, there is no suitable explanation for the baryon asymmetry of the universe~\cite{Sakharov:1967dj nor for the existence of dark matter~\cite{Arbey:2021gdg .}}
elements.sent-2411-016: These unresolved issues compel us to venture beyond the SM to address some or all of these phenomena.
elements.sent-2411-017: Assuming that the new physics beyond the SM exists, the SM can be thought of as an effective model, describing the particle interactions at the TeV scale, of an ultraviolet (UV)-complete model.
elements.sent-2411-018: In such UV-complete models, a natural consequence is the presence of one or more heavy particles.
elements.sent-2411-019: The models, which extend the SM gauge group, naturally predict additional vector bosons.
elements.sent-2411-020: On the other hand, the extension of the neutrino sector to address the neutrino mass and mixing puzzle opens up the possibility of heavier neutrinos.
elements.sent-2411-021: Such heavy particles can also arise without extending the gauge sector or particle sector in the SM.
elements.sent-2411-022: For example, the models with extra spatial dimensions, such as the Randall-Sundram (RS) model~\cite{Randall:1999ee,Randall:1999vf , predict heavier gauge bosons and heavier neutrinos through the Kaluza-Klein modes of the SM fields as a result of the compactification of the extra dimensions.}
elements.sent-2411-023: One of the compelling extensions to the Standard Model (SM) involves the introduction of additional heavy-charged vector bosons, commonly referred to as $W'$ bosons.
elements.sent-2411-024: These hypothetical particles are thought to be a counterpart of the SM $W$ boson, which mediates the weak force.
elements.sent-2411-025: Such a scenario arises in Left Right Symmetric model (LRSM) \cite{Mohapatra:1974gc,Mohapatra:1974hk,Senjanovic:1975rk,Senjanovic:1978ev , where there are two $SU(2)$ gauge groups, one responsible for the SM particle interaction and the other dominantly couples to the right-handed fermions.}
elements.sent-2411-026: A set of heavier neutrinos and gauge bosons are hence predicted, and coupling between $W'$ to SM or BSM particles is generated through mixing.
elements.sent-2411-027: Heavy gauge bosons also naturally arise in models with extra dimensions, such as the Randall-Sundram (RS) model.
elements.sent-2411-028: These heavier modes of the SM fields arise as Kaluza-Klein excitations due to the compactification of the extra spatial dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-029: Notably, in the LRSM case, the $W'$ coupling to a pair of fermions is expected to be right-chiral in nature, while the coupling is expected to be left-chiral in the models with extra dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-030: One of our queries is whether or not we can distinguish if such a discovery is attained at the high-luminosity LHC (HL-LHC) at 14 TeV centre-of-mass energy.
elements.sent-2411-031: Our method, in this work, does not consider a specific model, which usually considers either left-handed or right-handed coupling of $W'$.
elements.sent-2411-032: Rather, we take generic coupling of $W'$ to $q\bar{q'}$ and $\ell^\pm N$ ($N$ being neutrino or anti-neutrino).
elements.sent-2411-033: We then find signatures of $W'$ boson in the lepton plus a fat jet channel, where the fat jet comes from the decay of heavy neutrino.
elements.sent-2411-034: This fatjet also contains a lepton inside it.
elements.sent-2411-035: With the help of advanced jet substructure techniques and variables, we plan to isolate the embedded lepton as a subjet inside the fat jet.
elements.sent-2411-036: The Lepton Subjet Fraction ($LSF$) and Lepton Mass Drop ($LMD$) variables~\cite{Brust:2014gia are suggested to isolate such a lepton-subjet and would help us reduce the huge QCD background to a great extent.}
elements.sent-2411-037: At the same time, we can discriminate between left- and right-handed coupling hypotheses considering $W'$ boson is found in the mentioned channel.
elements.sent-2411-038: The unique points of our study can be summed up to the following points:.
elements.sent-2411-039: Our study focuses on the searches of heavy $W'$ signal in the channel with an energetic lepton and a fat jet, which embeds a lepton as a subjet.
elements.sent-2411-040: Jet Substructure technique is employed to find this leptonic subjet and to help reduce background to a significant extent.
elements.sent-2411-041: Polarization sensitive jet substructure observables are used to find the two different hypotheses of left- and right-handed coupling of $W'$ to the lepton and heavy neutrino pair.
elements.sent-2411-042: We further note that the search for $W'$ bosons remains one of the major focuses of the current run of the LHC.
elements.sent-2411-043: The experimental searches typically involve looking for high-mass resonances in events with leptons and missing energy, which could indicate the production and decay of a $W'$ boson.
elements.sent-2411-044: The absence of discovery sets lower bounds on the mass of $W'$ bosons, pushing the limits into the multi-TeV range.
elements.sent-2411-045: The current bound of $W'$ mass stands at somewhere around 5.5 TeV in the context of a sequential SM (SSM) scenario, where an SM-like coupling is considered for the $W'$~\cite{CMS:2021dzb,ATLAS:2019fgd .}
elements.sent-2411-046: In particular, the searches in the channel $\ell\ell j j$ with intermediate heavy neutrino have been performed, which provides an upper limit to the cross-section in this channel~\cite{ATLAS:2023cjo .}
elements.sent-2411-047: The constraint on heavy neutrino is, on the other hand, less constrained with a lower bound around 200~GeV.
elements.sent-2411-048: Despite the absence of the discoveries, the future runs of the LHC and other future colliders could be key platforms for discovering these particles.
elements.sent-2411-049: We, therefore, consider scenarios containing within the limits of these searches.

elements.sent-2411-061: For example, in the RS model, the $W'$ bosons can have couplings to heavy left-handed neutrinos and leptons.
elements.sent-2411-062: Thus, there exist a variety of possible couplings of $W'$ boson with left- or right-handed heavy neutrinos and leptons.

elements.sent-2411-064: In this, we focus on the proton-proton collisions at high-energy colliders at the High Luminosity Large Hadron Collider (HL-LHC), where a $W'$ boson could be produced and subsequently decay into a charged lepton and a heavy neutrino.
elements.sent-2411-065: The heavy neutrino could then decay into a charged lepton and a $W$ boson.
elements.sent-2411-066: In the scenario where $W'$ is much heavier than the heavy neutrino, the heavy neutrino produced is significantly boosted, resulting in a very small angle between the decay products of $N$.
elements.sent-2411-067: The hadronic decay $W$ boson thus leads to the final state containing a charged lepton and a fat jet, forming out the decays of boosted heavy neutrino and containing a lepton inside it.
elements.sent-2411-068: More importantly, the angular distribution due to the spin alignment of the decay products of heavy $W'$ provides information on the specific coupling of the $W'$ boson to a pair of fermions.
elements.sent-2411-069: The Lorentz structure at the production and decay vertices sets the distribution of the polarization-sensitive variables at the final state.
elements.sent-2411-070: For instance, if the $W'$ boson predominantly couples to the left(right)-chiral fermions, depending on the nature of coupling, the resulting decay products will exhibit corresponding left(right)-handed polarization, leading to distinctive asymmetries in their angular distribution.
elements.sent-2411-071: These asymmetries can be extracted from the heavy neutrino-induced fatjet, thanks to the advancement of jet substructure techniques and polarization-sensitive jet substructure observables.
elements.sent-2411-072: Therefore, it is possible to measure the polarization effect experimentally, which might deepen our insights into the nature of the $W'$ boson and the underlying interactions in Beyond Standard Model (BSM) physics.

elements.subsection-2411-001: A generic Lagrangian for $W'$ to left- and right-handed fermion
elements.sent-2411-075: In this paper, we are not restricting ourselves to a specific BSM scenario or model.
elements.sent-2411-076: Rather, we attempt to characterize the differences between the left and right-handed couplings of heavy vector bosons with the fermions and the possibility of detecting these types of couplings at the HL-LHC.
elements.sent-2411-077: Therefore, we add some additional terms in our SM lagrangian in a model-independent way, considering them as effective couplings.
elements.sent-2411-078: We also consider two additional particles, {\it viz.}
elements.sent-2411-079: The effective Lagrangian is, therefore, written as, \begin{eqnarray}\label{Lagrangian} \mathscr{L}_{\rm new} = g_{\ell L} {W'}_\mu \bar{N}_L \gamma^{\mu} \ell_L + g_{\ell R} W'_\mu \bar{N}_R \gamma^{\mu} \ell_R + g'_{\ell L} W \bar{N}_L \gamma^{\mu} \ell_L + g'_{\ell R} W \bar{N}_R \gamma^{\mu} \ell_R \nonumber +\ g_{q} {W'} (\bar{d}_R \gamma^{\mu} u_R + \bar{d}_L \gamma^{\mu} u_L) + \mathrm{h.c.}\end{eqnarray} Here, $g_{\ell L}$ denotes the coupling strength of the $W'$ to left-handed heavy neutrino and left-handed SM leptons, and $g_{\ell R}$ is the coupling strength of the same with right-handed heavy neutrino and right-handed SM leptons.
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\label{Lagrangian} \mathscr{L}_{\rm new} = g_{\ell L} {W'}_\mu \bar{N}_L \gamma^{\mu} \ell_L + g_{\ell R} W'_\mu \bar{N}_R \gamma^{\mu} \ell_R + g'_{\ell L} W \bar{N}_L \gamma^{\mu} \ell_L + g'_{\ell R} W \bar{N}_R \gamma^{\mu} \ell_R \nonumber +\ g_{q} {W'} (\bar{d}_R \gamma^{\mu} u_R + \bar{d}_L \gamma^{\mu} u_L) + h.c.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-080: Here, $N_L = P_L N$ and $N_R = P_R N$, whereas $P_L$ and $P_R$ are, respectively, the left and right projection matrices.
elements.sent-2411-081: There are two additional left and right couplings called $g'_{\ell L}$ and $g'_{\ell R}$, which are the couplings of SM $W$ boson with heavy neutrino and SM leptons.
elements.sent-2411-082: One can easily identify that the left and right-handed couplings of additional vector bosons will portray different polarization information through the final state decay products.
elements.sent-2411-083: The heavy vector boson can be generated at the LHC through $pp$-collision via the coupling $g_{q}$.
elements.sent-2411-084: After production, it can decay to a heavy neutrino and a charged lepton, followed by the heavy neutrino decay to SM $W$-boson and leptons.
elements.sent-2411-085: When we keep $g_{\ell L}$ and $g'_{\ell L}$ non-zero with right-handed couplings at zero, it will show the distribution of the left-handed polarized state of the heavy neutrino coupling to $W'$.

elements.sent-2411-134: In this section, we present a comprehensive signal-background analysis for a final state involving a fat jet and an isolated lepton at the HL-LHC at a centre-of-mass energy of $\sqrt{s = 14$~TeV, incorporating detector simulation.}
elements.sent-2411-135: As our focus is on studying this process under HL-LHC conditions, we assume a projected integrated luminosity of $\mathcal{L = 3$~ab$^{-1}$.}
elements.sent-2411-136: The signal process we consider involves the production of a heavy neutrino ($N$) via the reaction $p p\rightarrow W' \rightarrow N \ell$ at the LHC.
elements.sent-2411-137: The neutrino then decays through $N\rightarrow\ell q\bar{q'}$.
elements.sent-2411-138: Figure~\ref{fig:feyndiag depicts the Feynman diagram corresponding to the signal process under consideration.}
elements.sent-2411-139: In our scenario, the $W'$ is much heavier compared to its decay product $N$.
elements.sent-2411-140: Therefore, the resulting heavy neutrino $N$ is produced with a high boost.
elements.sent-2411-141: This momentum boost causes the three decay products of $N$ to be found in a narrow solid angle.
elements.sent-2411-142: When these decay products are reconstructed in the detector with a large-radius jet algorithm, they become clustered into a single, large-radius jet, also referred to as a fat jet.
elements.sent-2411-143: The fat jet formed from $N$'s decay possesses a unique three-pronged substructure.
elements.sent-2411-144: Two of the prongs correspond to the hadronic decays from the $W$ boson decay, while the third prong corresponds to the lepton produced in the decay of $N$.
elements.sent-2411-145: Due to the high boost of $N$, this lepton is contained within the radius of the fat jet, rather than being identified as an isolated lepton in the detector.
elements.sent-2411-146: This leptonic component within the fat jet is atypical for standard QCD jets, which are generally purely hadronic.
elements.sent-2411-147: Also, the fat jets arising from the particles in the SM do not usually contain lepton inside them.
elements.sent-2411-148: Therefore, this feature provides a distinguishing feature that helps separate this signal from background processes.
elements.sent-2411-149: Overall, this process yields a clean and identifiable final state that can be effectively differentiated from SM backgrounds.

The decay products of $N$ are highly collimated, forming a fat jet with a distinct substructure containing both leptonic and hadronic components.} \label{fig:feyndiag}
elements.sent-2411-150: To simulate this signal process, we employ a multi-step approach that accounts for the entire chain from parton-level event generation to the reconstructed final states as they would appear in the detector.
elements.sent-2411-151: First, we have implemented the model in {\tt FeynRules2.0}\,\cite{Alloul:2013bka and generated the Universal FeynRules Output (UFO)\,\cite{Degrande:2011ua files.}}
elements.sent-2411-152: Then, MadGraph5\_aMC@NLO~\cite{Alwall:2014hca is used to generate the hard scattering events at $\sqrt{s = 14$~TeV using the UFO files.}}
elements.sent-2411-153: Next, these events are passed through PYTHIA-8.3~\cite{Sjostrand:2006za,SJOSTRAND2008852 , which handles the parton showering and hadronization, producing hadronic final states keeping multi-parton interaction, initial- and final-state radiation switched on.}
elements.sent-2411-154: Finally, the events are processed with Delphes-3.5.0~\cite{deFavereau:2013fsa for detector simulation.}

elements.sent-2411-203: In the search for our aforementioned signal containing one fat jet and one high $p_T$ isolated lepton, several SM backgrounds need to be considered.

elements.sent-2411-287: This showcases that the jet substructure method is not only useful in finding signals by suppressing SM background, but it is also useful in constructing variables that help discriminate models with different polarization.
elements.sent-2411-288: Many new physics scenarios predict the existence of massive charged gauge boson $W'$ and its coupling to leptons and heavy neutrinos (N).
elements.sent-2411-289: In some scenarios, this coupling appears as $V+A$ (RH) and, in others, it can be considered to be $V-A$ (LH).
elements.sent-2411-290: The production of $W'$ and its decay to heavy neutrino and a lepton has been looked at the LHC in the $\ell\ell j j$ channel.
elements.sent-2411-291: In this work, we studied a different signal topology consisting of an energetic isolated lepton plus a fat jet, originating from the heavy $N$.
elements.sent-2411-292: This fat jet essentially embeds a lepton as a subjet along with two hadronic subjets inside it.
elements.sent-2411-293: By carefully analyzing the subjets through jet substructure-based methods and using appropriate variables, we have been able to isolate the signal by suppressing SM backgrounds.
elements.sent-2411-294: The variables $LSF$ (Lepton Subjet Fraction) and $LMD$ (Lepton Mass Drop) from the lepton subjet are particularly useful in suppressing huge QCD and $t\bar t$ backgrounds to achieve high signal significance.
elements.sent-2411-295: For the chosen benchmark points (see Table~\ref{Tab:benchmark ) with $W'$ mass ranging from 3 to 6 TeV and heavy neutrino mass ranging between 200 and 400 GeV, we have obtained $\sim 3\sigma$ to $\sim 5\sigma$ significance at the 14 TeV HL-LHC, considering 3~ab$^{-1}$ of integrated luminosity.}
elements.sent-2411-296: We further looked into the discrimination power between LH and RH coupling of $W'$ to lepton and heavy neutrino.
elements.sent-2411-297: We analyze this through the decay products of $N$.
elements.sent-2411-298: Since the decayed lepton from $N$ has a high spin analyzing power, appropriated variables constructed out of the lepton subjet, along with other subjets, provide a good discrimination power between the LH and RH configurations.
elements.sent-2411-299: We have used customized jet substructure-based polarization sensitive variables $z_\ell$, $z_\theta$ and $z_k$ to form the BDT score as a combined variable to help improve separation power.
elements.sent-2411-300: The discrimination score is presented in terms of confidence level by using the $CL_s$-type profile likelihood estimator method, in which the significance of excluding one hypothesis in favour of the other is obtained.
elements.sent-2411-301: For the chosen benchmark points, we were able to achieve 2$\sigma$ to 3$\sigma$ significance of excluding the LH hypothesis in favour of RH configuration and {\it vice versa in the signal region.}
elements.sent-2411-302: Thus the 14 TeV HL-LHC with 3~ab$^{-1}$ integrated luminosity not only appears to be a good platform to probe the production of $W'$ in the lepton plus fat jet channel, it also has the potential to discriminate between LH and RH nature of coupling if such a discovery is achieved.
